title: Oxidative coupling polymerization of 4-methyltriphenylamine
abstract:
Macromol. Rapid Commun. 20, 103–106 (1999)
103
Oxidative coupling polymerization of 4-methyltriphenylamine
Kenji Ogino*1, Aiko Kanegae1, Ryoji Yamaguchi1, Hisaya Sato1, Jan Kurjata2
1 Department of Material Systems Engineering, Faculty of Technology, Tokyo University of Agriculture and
Technology, Koganei, Tokyo 184, Japan
2 Center of Molecular and Macromolecular Studies of the Polish Academy of Science, Sienkiewicza 112,
90-3 63 Lodz, Poland
(Received: October 9, 1998; revised: November 12, 1998)
SUMMARY: 4-Methyltriphenylamine was oxidatively polymerized in chloroform (CL) or propylene carbon-
ate (PC) in the presence of ferric chloride (FeCl3) at 508C. Polymerization proceeded more rapidly and the
molecular weight of the resulting polymer was higher in CL than in PC due to the higher oxidation potential
of the solution. The linear structure was confirmed by 13C NMR, and the resulting polymer exhibited high
thermal stability and electrochemical activity.
Introduction
Triarylamine derivatives have been widely used as hole-
transport compounds in organic photoconductors and
electroluminescent devices. Their hole-transport ability is
based on the fact that they are easily oxidized to form
stable radical cations. Triarylamine oligomers and dendri-
mers are most attractive compounds for several applica-
tions because of their thermal stability and relatively low
ionization potential1, 2). The synthesis of this type of mole-
cules has been limited by the modest yield of the copper-
mediated Ullmann reaction3). New palladium-catalyzed
methods also have been applied to synthesize high mole-
cular weight triarylamine dendrimers4). In these reactions,
aryl bromides are coupled with arylamines or diaryl-
amines5, 6). Nickel-catalyzed coupling reaction between
aryl bromide and aryl Grignard reagent has been also
applied to synthesize a branched poly(triphenylamine)7).
Recently we reported that triarylamine dimers like N,N9-
diphenyl-N,N9-bis(4-methylphenyl)benzidine
(p-TPD)
were simply and selectively prepared in high yield by
chemical oxidative coupling of triarylamines using ferric
chloride8). In this work, an oxidative coupling reaction is
applied to obtain a linear poly(triarylamine). This type of
polymer would be a new important class of material in a
variety of fields because it should have thermal stability
and processibility which are characteristics of polymeric
compounds, and have a new type of conjugated system.
Experimental part
The starting material, 4-methyltriphenylamine (MTPA) was
synthesized from commercially available diphenylamine and
4-iodotoluene by Ullmann condensation3). Polymerization
was carried out at 508C in the presence of 4 mol equivalent
of ferric chloride under nitrogen atmosphere in chloroform
(CL) or propylene carbonate (PC), which were convention-
ally purified. As we reported, the low temperature (0–208C)
and the use of a larger amount of ferric chloride preferably
yielded a dimer (p-TPD) in A90% yield8). Tab. 1 summarizes
the polymerization conditions. After the polymerization, the
reaction mixture was poured into methanol to recover the
product, and the precipitate was washed with methanol
twice. Collected dark brown powder was dissolved in CL,
and the insoluble part if existed was removed by filtration.
The filtrate was washed with water and aqueous ammonium
hydroxide, then the color of the CL layer changed to light
brown. After drying with anhydrous magnesium sulfate, the
concentrated solution was poured into acetone to recover
light brown powders.
Results and discussion
As shown in Tab. 1, the polymerization reaction proceeds
more rapidly in CL than in PC. This is mainly due to the
difference of the oxidation potential in solution (1.10 V in
CL, 0.95 V in PC versus Ag/AgCl, 0.1 M). Only 30 min
polymerization provided a product in 68% yield, and the
recovered polymer was completely soluble in CL. After
polymerization, most of the product was precipitated in
CL. When the polymerization time was 1.5 h, the yield
reached was almost quantitative (96%) even though the
reaction system was heterogeneous. In this case, 58% of
total polymer was insoluble in CL, but 35% of this
chloroform insoluble part was soluble in o-dichloroben-
zene at 1008C. It is considered that this partial insolubi-
lity is not due to side reactions causing crosslinking, but
to the fact that higher molecular weight species is inher-
ently insoluble in CL. That is why the obtained polymers
(CL soluble parts) had relatively low molecular weights.
Therefore, it is expected that the introduction of a longer
and flexible side chain would increase the solubility and
help for obtaining higher molecular weight polymers.
The CL soluble part could be fractionated based on the
solubility in toluene, and the toluene insoluble part (2-2)
Macromol. Rapid Commun. 20, No. 3
i WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69451 Weinheim 1999
1022-1336/99/0303–0103$17.50+.50/0
 104
K. Ogino, A. Kanegae, R. Yamaguchi, H. Sato, J. Kurjata
had a higher molecular weight than the soluble part (2-1).
It was found that the polymerization reaction efficiently
proceeds even in a heterogeneous system and solubility
of the polymer is dependent on the molecular weight. The
reaction system using PC as a solvent remained homoge-
neous even after 18 h. The yield reached 40% after 1.5 h,
and gradually increased with the reaction time. In the
case of PC, all the recovered products were soluble in
CL. Although the molecular weight also increased
slightly with the reaction time, the products mainly con-
sisted of oligomers with less than 5 monomer units. The
low molecular weight character of the oligomers obtained

Oxidative coupling polymerization of 4-methyltriphenylamine
Kenji Ogino*1, Aiko Kanegae1, Ryoji Yamaguchi1, Hisaya Sato1, Jan Kurjata2
1 Department of Material Systems Engineering, Faculty of Technology, Tokyo University of Agriculture and
Technology, Koganei, Tokyo 184, Japan
2 Center of Molecular and Macromolecular Studies of the Polish Academy of Science, Sienkiewicza 112,
(Received: October 9, 1998; revised: November 12, 1998)

total polymer was insoluble in CL, but 35% of this

zene at 1008C. It is considered that this partial insolubi-

and flexible side chain would increase the solubility and

Oxidative coupling polymerization of 4-methyltriphenylamine
in PC is also attributed to the lower oxidation potential of
ferric ions in PC compared with that in CL.
Fig. 1 shows 13C NMR spectra of the obtained poly-
mers. As shown in Fig. 1(b), mainly 8 signals were
observed in the aromatic region for a high molecular
weight polymer (2-2), which means that coupling reac-
tions occurred exclusively at para position of unsubsti-
tuted N-phenyl rings, and a linear structure as shown in
Fig. 1 was obtained. Moreover, the chemical shift of each
signal is reasonable assuming the linear structure and tak-
ing the assignment of a dimer, p-TPD, into account8). The
additional small signals 10–18 observed clearly in low
molecular weight polymer (1) are assignable to monomer
units at the polymer chain end. These assignments are
confirmed by the decreasing intensity of these signals
with increasing of molecular weight.
Not only high molecular weight polymers obtained in
CL but also oligomers obtained in PC could be processed
to thin films by both spin-coating and wire-bar-coating
from CL solutions. Although resulting films showed
excellent morphological stability, they are rather brittle
due to the low molecular weight character of the pro-
p-TPD shows a glass transition at 528C9). This low
glass transition temperature limits the operating tempera-
ture of the devices10, 11). As expected, the glass transition
temperature increases with increasing molecular weight.
Differential scanning calorimetry measurements revealed
that oligomer (5) has a glass transition temperature of
1498C, and polymer (2) showed a glass transition at
2078C. According to thermogravimetry curves, polymers
(1) and (2) started weight loss at ca. 4008C under nitro-
gen flow. This outstanding thermal stability also renders
advantages in various applications.
UVabsorption spectra of MTPA and the dimer (p-TPD)
solution in CL had absorption maxima at 303 and
354 nm, respectively, assignable to the p-p* transition
resulting from the conjugation between the aromatic rings
and nitrogen atoms. Oligomer (5) showed an absorption
maximum at 364 nm. In the spectrum of polymer (2), the
absorption maximum further shifted to 374 nm. These
observations are probably due to the fact that a weak con-
jugated interaction exists between biphenylene units and
nitrogen atoms as the number of repeating units increases.
No significant difference was observed between polymers
(2-1) and (2-2). A similar red-shift has been observed in a
branched poly(triphenylamine)7).
The redox behavior was investigated with cyclic vol-
tammetry conducted for the cast film on a platinum work-
ing electrode in dry acetonitrile containing tetrabutylam-
monium perchlorate (0.1 M) as an electrolyte under nitro-
gen atmosphere. As shown in Fig. 2, the cyclic voltam-
mogram of polymer (2) shows an oxidation wave of
which a peak top is at 1.05 V (versus Ag/AgCl) and a
reduction wave at 0.8 V with a shoulder at ca. 0.7 V. As
reported for polydiphenylamine12) or poly(4-aminobiphe-
nyl)13), the synthesized polymer should also be oxidized
reversibly in two separate steps, although only one oxida-
tion peak was observed. Redox peaks observed here are
much broader than those observed in an acrylate polymer
containing p-TPD in the side chain showing a well-
defined couple of redox peaks14). This observation indi-
cates that in partially oxidized polymer chains, the oxi-
dized triarylamine unit has an electrochemical effect on
the adjacent units, because of a weak conjugation
between biphenylene units and nitrogen atoms. Similar
peak broadening was observed for polydiphenylamine
with a higher number of repeating units12). The polymer
films displayed an electrochromic property, as they were
transformed from the colorless neutral form to the reddish
In summary, a simple oxidative coupling reaction of
MTPA provided linear polymers where MTPA units are
connected exclusively at p-position of the two unsubsti-
tuted two N-phenyl rings. The resulting polymers showed
good solubility in CL and higher glass transition tempera-
tures compared with the dimer (p-TPD). The investiga-
tion of electrochemical properties suggests that our novel
polymers have a great potential as a new type of hole-
